content.text: Immutable Build Artifacts for GenAI Services

An immutable build artifact is one that cannot change after it is created. For GenAI services, immutability extends beyond the compiled application: it must include the system prompt files loaded at startup, the model version pin, and the version envelope tying these together. Any artifact that can change after the eval gate passes invalidates the evaluation.

This document covers what belongs inside the immutable artifact bundle for a GenAI service, how to build and sign it, how to bake prompts into the image, and how to verify immutability at each promotion gate.

attrs.alt: Immutable artifact bundle components for GenAI services

content.text: Why GenAI Artifacts Need Stronger Immutability Guarantees
Conventional application artifacts must not allow code changes between environments. GenAI artifacts have an additional risk: the
prompt files

that determine model behaviour may be loaded from disk at runtime, outside the image. If prompt files are mounted as ConfigMaps or read from object storage at boot, a different prompt can be served in production than the one that passed the staging eval.
Risk
Conventional app
GenAI app
Code change between envs
Prevented by digest pinning
Prevented by digest pinning
Prompt change between envs
Not applicable
Must be baked into image or pinned by hash
Model version drift
Not applicable
Must be pinned to a dated version string
Dependency version drift
Prevented by lock file in image
Same — lock file in image
Base image update
Use FROM name@sha256 to prevent
Same — use FROM name@sha256 to prevent
Loading prompts from a ConfigMap at runtime makes them mutable. The ConfigMap can be updated independently of a deployment, silently changing model behaviour without triggering any CI gate.
Baking Prompts into the Docker Image
The safest approach is to copy prompt files into the image at build time. The prompts directory becomes part of the image layer and is covered by the SHA256 digest:

# Dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim@sha256:abcdef1234...   # pin base image by digest

WORKDIR /app

# Pin dependencies before copying application code
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY app/ ./app/

# Bake prompts into the image — these are immutable after this layer
COPY prompts/ ./prompts/

# Record the prompt version inside the image
ARG PROMPT_VERSION=unknown
ARG APP_VERSION=unknown
ARG LLM_MODEL=unknown
ARG GIT_SHA=unknown
ARG EVAL_RUN_ID=unknown

# Embed in a file that the app reads at startup
RUN echo "{"prompt_version": "$PROMPT_VERSION", "app_version": "$APP_VERSION", "model": "$LLM_MODEL", "git_sha": "$GIT_SHA", "eval_run_id": "$EVAL_RUN_ID"}" > /app/version_envelope.json

# Label the image with the same metadata (visible via docker inspect)
LABEL app.version="$APP_VERSION"       prompt.version="$PROMPT_VERSION"       llm.model="$LLM_MODEL"       git.sha="$GIT_SHA"

CMD ["python", "-m", "app.main"]

# Build with all version args injected from CI environment
docker build   --build-arg APP_VERSION="$APP_VERSION"   --build-arg PROMPT_VERSION="$(cat prompts/.version)"   --build-arg LLM_MODEL="$(jq -r .model config/model_versions.json)"   --build-arg GIT_SHA="$GITHUB_SHA"   --build-arg EVAL_RUN_ID="$EVAL_RUN_ID"   --no-cache   -t myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION .
Generating the Software Bill of Materials

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) records every software component included in the image — Python packages, system libraries, and the prompt files themselves. It is required for supply-chain security compliance (SLSA level 2+) and enables rapid triage when a vulnerability is disclosed in a dependency:

# Generate SBOM using Syft immediately after build
# Install: curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/syft/main/install.sh | sh

syft myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION   -o spdx-json=sbom.spdx.json   -o cyclonedx-json=sbom.cyclonedx.json

# Store the SBOM keyed by image digest so it can be retrieved by digest later
DIGEST=$(docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION | cut -d@ -f2)
aws s3 cp sbom.spdx.json "s3://my-sboms/$DIGEST/sbom.spdx.json"
aws s3 cp sbom.cyclonedx.json "s3://my-sboms/$DIGEST/sbom.cyclonedx.json"
echo "SBOM stored for digest: $DIGEST"
Signing the Artifact with Cosign

Cosign provides keyless signing using OIDC tokens, which works natively in GitHub Actions without managing private keys. The signature is stored in the container registry alongside the image and verified at deployment time:

# .github/workflows/build.yml (signing step)
  sign-artifact:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write    # required for keyless signing
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@v3

      - name: Sign the image digest
        env:
          DIGEST: ${{ needs.build.outputs.image_digest }}
        run: |
          cosign sign --yes myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION@$DIGEST

      - name: Attach eval attestation
        run: |
          cosign attest --yes             --predicate eval/eval_results.json             --type https://myapp.example.com/attestation/eval/v1             myregistry/myapp:$APP_VERSION@$DIGEST

# Verify at deployment time — add to gate scripts
cosign verify   --certificate-identity-regexp "https://github.com/myorg/myapp/.*"   --certificate-oidc-issuer "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"   myregistry/myapp:1.3.2@sha256:abc123...

# Verify eval attestation is present
cosign verify-attestation   --type https://myapp.example.com/attestation/eval/v1   --certificate-identity-regexp "https://github.com/myorg/myapp/.*"   --certificate-oidc-issuer "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"   myregistry/myapp:1.3.2@sha256:abc123... |   jq '.payload | @base64d | fromjson | .predicate'
Verifying Immutability at Each Gate
At each promotion gate, a script verifies that the artifact being promoted matches the digest recorded at build time and that no mutable components have changed:
Handling Prompt Updates Without a Full Rebuild
Some teams want to update prompts more frequently than the full build cycle allows. Two safe approaches exist — both avoid the mutable ConfigMap anti-pattern:
Approach A: Thin Prompt Layer

Build a separate "prompt image" that contains only the prompt files, and compose it with the application image using a multi-stage build. Updating prompts triggers only a re-build of the prompt layer (seconds) rather than the full application rebuild:

# Stage 1: Build application (slow, infrequent)
FROM python:3.12-slim@sha256:... AS app-layer
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY app/ ./app/

# Stage 2: Prompts (fast, frequent updates)
FROM scratch AS prompt-layer
COPY prompts/ /prompts/

# Stage 3: Compose — prompt layer is independently cacheable
FROM app-layer
COPY --from=prompt-layer /prompts/ ./prompts/
ARG PROMPT_VERSION=unknown
RUN echo "{"prompt_version": "$PROMPT_VERSION"}" > /app/version_envelope.json
Approach B: Prompt Registry with Content Addressing

Store prompt versions in an object store keyed by their SHA256 hash. At startup, the application fetches the exact prompt version specified in the version envelope. The hash in the envelope is immutable; only a new build can change which hash is referenced:

# app/prompt_loader.py
import hashlib, json, os, pathlib, urllib.request

PROMPT_REGISTRY_BASE = os.environ["PROMPT_REGISTRY_URL"]   # e.g. s3://my-prompts/

def load_prompt(name: str, expected_hash: str) -> str:
    url = f"{PROMPT_REGISTRY_BASE}/{name}/{expected_hash}.txt"
    content = urllib.request.urlopen(url).read().decode()
    actual_hash = hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest()
    if actual_hash != expected_hash:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Prompt hash mismatch for {name}: "
            f"expected {expected_hash}, got {actual_hash}"
        )
    return content

def load_all_prompts(envelope_path: str = "/app/version_envelope.json") -> dict[str, str]:
    envelope = json.loads(pathlib.Path(envelope_path).read_text())
    prompt_hashes = envelope.get("prompt_hashes", {})
    return {name: load_prompt(name, h) for name, h in prompt_hashes.items()}
Reproducible Builds and the Lock File Strategy
A reproducible build is one where the same source inputs always produce the same artifact. For Python GenAI applications, non-reproducibility commonly comes from:
Unpinned dependencies
pip install openai
installs the latest version at build time, which changes as the library is updated.
Unpinned base image
FROM python:3.12
can silently change when the image maintainer pushes a new patch.
Non-deterministic package build
— some packages with C extensions build differently on different hardware.
The solution is a three-layer pinning strategy:

# Step 1: Generate a fully-resolved lock file from abstract requirements
pip-compile requirements.in   --generate-hashes   --output-file requirements.txt
# requirements.txt now contains exact versions AND SHA256 hashes
# for every package, including transitive dependencies.

# Step 2: Install strictly from the lock file (no network resolution)
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt
# --require-hashes refuses any package not in requirements.txt,
# blocking supply-chain attacks via dependency confusion.

# Step 3: Pin the base image digest in Dockerfile
# Find the digest:
docker pull python:3.12-slim
docker inspect python:3.12-slim --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}'
# sha256:abcdef1234...
# Use in Dockerfile:
# FROM python:3.12-slim@sha256:abcdef1234...

# CI: verify the lock file is current (no unresolved drift)
# Run this in the validate stage before build:
pip-compile requirements.in   --generate-hashes   --output-file /tmp/requirements_check.txt   --quiet
diff requirements.txt /tmp/requirements_check.txt ||   (echo "requirements.txt is out of date — run pip-compile to update" && exit 1)
Model Version Pinning
LLM provider APIs often offer a
latest
alias (e.g.
gpt-4o
) that silently redirects to the newest model version as the provider releases updates. This is a mutation risk: the model behaviour changes without any artifact change.

# app/config.py
import json, pathlib

_MODEL_VERSIONS = json.loads(
    (pathlib.Path(__file__).parent.parent / "config/model_versions.json").read_text()
)

CHAT_MODEL: str      = _MODEL_VERSIONS["chat_model"]
JUDGE_MODEL: str     = _MODEL_VERSIONS["judge_model"]
EMBEDDING_MODEL: str = _MODEL_VERSIONS["embedding_model"]

# Usage in the app:
# from app.config import CHAT_MODEL
# client.chat.completions.create(model=CHAT_MODEL, ...)
A PR that updates
config/model_versions.json
must trigger the full eval gate regardless of which path-filter triggers are configured. Add
config/model_versions.json
to the prompt-changed path filter so the eval gate always runs on model version changes.
Scanning Artifacts for Vulnerabilities
The SBOM enables continuous vulnerability scanning without rebuilding the image. Scan the SBOM against known CVE databases on a schedule and alert when new vulnerabilities are disclosed:

# .github/workflows/vulnerability-scan.yml
name: Artifact Vulnerability Scan

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'   # daily at 6 AM
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Download latest SBOM from registry
        run: |
          # Get the digest of the current production image
          DIGEST=$(kubectl get deployment myapp -n production             -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}' | cut -d@ -f2)
          aws s3 cp "s3://my-sboms/$DIGEST/sbom.cyclonedx.json" sbom.json

      - name: Scan SBOM with Grype
        run: |
          # Install: curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/grype/main/install.sh | sh
          grype sbom:./sbom.json             --fail-on high             --output json > grype_results.json

      - name: Alert on critical findings
        if: failure()
        run: |
          python scripts/alert_vulnerabilities.py grype_results.json

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: vulnerability-scan-results
          path: grype_results.json
Artifact Retention and Cleanup Policy
Immutable artifacts must be retained long enough for rollback and incident investigation but cleaned up to control storage costs. Define the policy by environment:
Environment
Retention Policy
Reason
Development
7 days
Short-lived experiments; low rollback value
Staging
30 days
May need to reproduce staging-specific issues
Pre-production
90 days
Regulatory audit window
Production
1 year
Incident investigation and compliance requirement
Production (hotfix)
3 years
Extended retention for safety-critical patches

# scripts/cleanup_old_artifacts.sh
# Deletes images older than retention policy for a given environment tag

NAMESPACE="$1"             # dev | staging | preprod | prod
RETENTION_DAYS="${2:-30}"  # default 30 days
REGISTRY="myregistry/myapp"

# List all tags for this namespace
TAGS=$(aws ecr list-images --repository-name myapp   --filter tagStatus=TAGGED --query 'imageIds[*].imageTag' --output text)

CUTOFF=$(date -d "$RETENTION_DAYS days ago" +%s)
for TAG in $TAGS; do
  if [[ "$TAG" == *"-$NAMESPACE"* ]]; then
    PUSHED=$(aws ecr describe-images --repository-name myapp       --image-ids imageTag=$TAG       --query 'imageDetails[0].imagePushedAt' --output text)
    PUSHED_EPOCH=$(date -d "$PUSHED" +%s)
    if [ $PUSHED_EPOCH -lt $CUTOFF ]; then
      echo "Deleting $TAG (pushed $PUSHED)"
      aws ecr batch-delete-image --repository-name myapp         --image-ids imageTag=$TAG
    fi
  fi
done
Summary

Immutable build artifacts for GenAI services require extending standard Docker immutability practices to cover prompt files and model version pins. The artifact that passes the eval gate must be byte-for-byte identical to the artifact deployed in production.
Pin the base image by SHA256 digest in the Dockerfile
FROM
instruction.
Bake prompt files into the image at build time; never load them from a mutable ConfigMap at runtime.
Record the version envelope (app version, prompt version, model, eval run ID, git SHA) inside the image.
Generate an SBOM with Syft and store it keyed by image digest for supply-chain compliance.
Sign the image with Cosign and attach the eval attestation to the same digest.
Verify the digest and version envelope at each promotion gate before allowing the artifact to advance.
For high-frequency prompt updates, use a thin prompt layer or content-addressed prompt registry rather than a mutable ConfigMap.
